text:
RADIOLOGY REPORT

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
MRN: [REDACTED]
EXAMINATION: (1) Technetium-99m Methylene Diphosphonate (MDP) Whole Body Bone Scan with SPECT/CT of the Lumbar Spine and Pelvis; (2) CT of the Chest, Abdomen, and Pelvis with Intravenous Contrast
INDICATION: 68-year-old male with newly diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5. PSA 187 ng/mL. Staging evaluation.
COMPARISON: No prior imaging available for comparison.

---

TECHNETIUM-99m MDP WHOLE BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY WITH SPECT/CT

TECHNIQUE: Planar whole body images were acquired in anterior and posterior projections approximately 3 hours following the intravenous administration of 25 mCi of Tc-99m MDP. Supplemental SPECT/CT images of the lumbar spine and pelvis were obtained to better characterize areas of abnormality.

FINDINGS:

Skull and Cervical Spine: No abnormal foci of radiotracer uptake. Normal calvarium and facial bone activity.

Thoracic Spine: Physiologic distribution of tracer throughout the thoracic vertebral column. No focal abnormalities identified. Normal rib uptake bilaterally.

Lumbar Spine: There are two discrete foci of markedly increased radiotracer uptake at the L3 and L4 vertebral bodies. These demonstrate intense, focal uptake in a pattern consistent with osteoblastic metastatic involvement. SPECT/CT correlation confirms the lesions are centered within the vertebral bodies with involvement extending into the posterior elements at L3. No associated cortical destruction to suggest purely lytic activity. No significant epidural extension on the limited CT component.

Pelvis: A well-defined focus of moderately increased tracer uptake is present in the right iliac wing, mid-portion, without involvement of the sacroiliac joint. Additionally, there is a distinct focus of increased uptake at the right femoral neck, immediately below the femoral head, with uptake slightly asymmetric to the contralateral side. SPECT/CT demonstrates both lesions to be intraosseous without frank cortical breakthrough, though the femoral neck lesion warrants clinical attention given anatomical weight-bearing implications.

Sacrum and Coccyx: No pathologic uptake identified. Normal symmetrical sacroiliac joint activity.

Upper Extremities: No abnormal foci. Symmetric uptake at the shoulders, elbows, and wrists consistent with mild degenerative change of no clinical concern in this context.

Lower Extremities: The right femoral neck lesion described above. Remainder of the bilateral lower extremities show no additional sites of abnormal uptake. Normal uptake at the knees and ankles bilaterally.

Soft Tissues: Physiologic renal excretion of tracer with normal bilateral renal uptake. Bladder activity present as expected. No focal soft tissue uptake of significance.

IMPRESSION — BONE SCAN:
1. Multiple foci of increased radiotracer uptake consistent with osseous metastatic disease in the clinical setting of high-grade prostate adenocarcinoma. Sites of involvement include the L3 and L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck.
2. No evidence of widespread or "superscan" pattern of metastatic disease.
3. The right femoral neck lesion, while appearing intraosseous without cortical breakthrough on SPECT/CT, is noted in a weight-bearing location and warrants orthopedic/clinical review.

---

CT OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT acquisition of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of intravenous iohexol contrast (100 mL, Omnipaque 350). Oral contrast was administered. Axial images were obtained at 1.25 mm slice thickness with multiplanar reconstructions in coronal and sagittal planes.

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. No focal consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or parenchymal nodule identified. Specifically, no discrete pulmonary nodule suspicious for metastatic disease is seen. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum: The mediastinum is midline. Cardiac silhouette is within normal limits for the patient's age. No mediastinal or hilar adenopathy. The aorta and great vessels are patent without aneurysmal change, though mild atherosclerotic calcification is present, compatible with the patient's age and cardiovascular risk profile. No pericardial effusion.

Chest Wall and Osseous Structures: Mild multilevel degenerative changes in the thoracic spine. No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions in the visualized ribs or sternum. No soft tissue mass along the chest wall.

CT ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and morphology. No focal hepatic lesions identified. The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously on portal venous phase imaging. The hepatic veins and portal vein are patent.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is present, wall within normal limits, no cholelithiasis. No biliary ductal dilatation.

Pancreas: Normal pancreatic morphology, no focal mass or ductal abnormality. No peripancreatic inflammation.

Spleen: Spleen is normal in size (approximately 10.4 cm in craniocaudal dimension) and homogeneous in attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable without focal mass or nodularity. No adrenal metastatic lesions.

Kidneys and Ureters: The kidneys are symmetric in size and demonstrate normal parenchymal enhancement. No hydronephrosis, no renal calculi, and no focal renal lesions. The ureters are not dilated.

Retroperitoneum and Mesenteric Nodes: The retroperitoneal nodal chains appear unremarkable. No pathologically enlarged para-aortic or aortocaval lymph nodes. A few small retroperitoneal nodes measuring up to 7 mm (short axis) are present, well below the threshold for concern. Mesenteric fat is normal without lymphadenopathy.

Bowel: No bowel obstruction or wall thickening. Appendix is visualized and appears unremarkable.

Ascites: None.

CT PELVIS:

Prostate and Seminal Vesicles: The prostate gland is enlarged, measuring approximately 45 cc in volume. There is asymmetric bulging of the right posterolateral contour of the prostate with loss of the normal fat plane between the prostate and right seminal vesicle, consistent with extracapsular extension. Both seminal vesicles appear thickened and asymmetrically hyperdense relative to expected normal signal, with the right seminal vesicle more prominently involved. The left seminal vesicle is also abnormal, with loss of the intervening fat plane at the prostate-seminal vesicle junction. These findings are consistent with bilateral seminal vesicle invasion.

Bladder: No bladder wall thickening. No intravesical mass or filling defect. Bladder is mildly underdistended but appears normal.

Rectum: No perirectal mass or lymphadenopathy. Rectum is normal in caliber.

Lymphadenopathy: A right obturator lymph node is enlarged, measuring 1.2 cm in short-axis dimension, with a rounded, mildly heterogeneous appearance and subtle central loss of the fatty hilum, morphologically suspicious for nodal metastatic involvement in this clinical context. No contralateral obturator, external iliac, or common iliac lymphadenopathy. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Vessels: Normal caliber iliac vessels bilaterally. Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the aortic bifurcation and proximal common iliacs.

Osseous Structures: The previously described sclerotic foci at L3, L4, the right iliac wing, and the right femoral neck correspond to the areas of increased tracer uptake seen on bone scan, with the CT appearances showing mixed sclerotic and mildly expansile character consistent with osteoblastic metastatic disease. No pathologic fracture.

Soft Tissues: No pelvic soft tissue masses separate from the prostate. Mild bilateral inguinal fat prominence without discrete lymphadenopathy.

IMPRESSION — CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS:
1. Locally advanced prostate adenocarcinoma with features of extracapsular extension and bilateral seminal vesicle invasion, as described.
2. Solitary right obturator lymph node enlargement with morphologic features suspicious for nodal metastatic involvement (short axis 1.2 cm).
3. No evidence of visceral metastatic disease — specifically, the liver, lungs, and adrenal glands are clear of metastatic lesions.
4. No additional pelvic, retroperitoneal, or mediastinal adenopathy identified.
5. Osseous findings at L3, L4, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck correspond to sites of abnormal radiotracer uptake on the concurrent bone scan, consistent with osteoblastic skeletal metastases.

OVERALL IMPRESSION:
Imagings are consistent with metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma with skeletal metastases involving L3, L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck; and regional nodal disease with a suspicious right obturator lymph node (1.2 cm). No visceral (hepatic, pulmonary, or adrenal) metastatic disease is identified. Locally advanced primary disease with extracapsular extension and bilateral seminal vesicle invasion is confirmed on CT.

Results communicated to the referring oncologist.

Interpreting Radiologist: [SIGNATURE REDACTED]
Dictated and finalized electronically.
